text: Kings. Greek, "they sent to him ambassadors, with proposals for peace, saying: Behold we, the servants of Nabuchodonosor, the great king, lay before thee; use us as thou thinkest best," etc. These proposals were made by those on the sea-coast, from Sidon and Tyre to Ascalon, from whom Holofernes took "the choicest men," being received with honour, ver. 10. (Haydock)<hr>Syria, etc. These are not specified in the Greek.<hr>Sobal, or Soba, (1 Kings 14:47.) near Damascus, where Ptolemy (v.) places Samoulis.<hr>Lybia seems too remote, and had not been attacked: some therefore would read Lycia, or Lygia, (Herodotus 7:72.) or rather Lydia, (Calmet) which Holofernes had "cut in pieces," according to the Greek, Judith 2:13. (Haydock)

Great. This was his usual title, Daniel 2:30., and 4 Kings 18:19. (Calmet)<hr>Slavery. Yet they make an unconditional submission. (Haydock)<hr>It is better for us to submit to servitude than to be slaughtered like our brethren. (Menochius)
Sight, at thy disposal, Genesis 13:9., and Proverbs 15:11.
Lights. Torches were used to testify joy, 2 Machabees 4:22.
Though. Greek, "He demolished all their borders, and cut down their groves."

Called. Greek, "Worshipped by all nations, and that all their tongues and tribes might call him god." (Haydock)<hr>Behold to what lengths ambition may be carried. If he had only claimed a place among the other pagan deities, his title was as good as theirs. (Calmet)<hr>He is a figure of Antichrist; for whom all heretics pave the way, 2 Thessalonians 2:(Worthington)

Apamea, on the river Orontes, between which and the river Eleutherus, lay the Mesopotamia here mentioned.<hr>Idumeans, or to "Rama," near Gabaa, which was on the north of Jerusalem; whereas Idumea lay far to the south. (Calmet)<hr>Yet (Haydock) Gabaa may designate a mountainous country, as 1 Kings 7:1., (Menochius) and there is no reason why Idumea might not have felt the power of Holofernes, as well as Madian, etc. (Haydock)<hr>The order of conquests is not observed, (Menochius) for while the commander-in-chief was in one place, his generals were dispersed into different parts. He had now advanced as far as Gabaa of Saul, (Haydock) when meditating a serious attack upon Egypt, he thought proper to concentrate his forces; little expecting to meet with such a check at Bethulia. (Calmet)<hr>Greek, "He came to Esdrelon, near Dotaia, (or Dothaim, north of Sichem) which is opposite the great saw of Judea," or the range of mountains of Ephraim. (Calmet)<hr>Old Vulgate, "of Juda," dividing it from Idumea. The kings of Israel had fortified the defiles, 3 Kings 15:17.
Took. Greek, "encamped between Gaba and Scythopolis, (Bethsan, Josue 17:11.) and he was there a month of days, to collect all the burden of his army."
